nativeRange.habitat: Sandy or silty reef-base areas where the shrimp excavates and maintains burrows, often shared with a partner goby.
nativeRange.biotope: Tropical coral-reef rubble and sand interfaces

decorPreferences: fine aragonite sand bed at least 5 cm deep for burrowing
small rubble and shell fragments for burrow shoring
live rock with stable base (rock placed directly on glass, not on sand)
tightly fitted lid to discourage escape

diet.notes: Has been documented taking meaty frozen foods (mysis, brine, finely chopped seafood), sinking pellets, and detritus scavenged from the substrate. Generally advised to target-feed near the burrow entrance once or twice weekly.

avoidWith: other pistol shrimp (conspecific aggression in confined space)
small ornamental shrimp the snap can stun
very small fish that may be ambushed near the burrow

summary: The Candy Stripe Pistol Shrimp is a small Indo-Pacific snapping shrimp, generally considered reef-safe and best known for forming close burrow-sharing partnerships with shrimp gobies. Adults reach 3–5 cm and are well suited to reef tanks of 75 litres or more.
careNotes:
The Candy Stripe Pistol Shrimp is sold in the aquarium trade as Alpheus sp. because the identity is rarely pinned down to species level at point of sale. The shrimp most often sold under this name is thought to be Alpheus randalli (sometimes called Randall's Pistol Shrimp), identified by its distinctive red-and-white banded body, though a few morphologically similar relatives — including A. soror and A. astrinx — may enter the trade under the same label.

This shrimp is generally kept at 22–27°C with reef-typical pH (8.1–8.4) and specific gravity around 1.023–1.026. A fine sand bed at least 5 cm deep is strongly recommended so the shrimp can excavate a burrow. Rockwork should rest directly on the aquarium glass rather than on the sand, because the shrimp will tunnel beneath anything it can reach and can destabilise improperly placed rocks. The shrimp tends to be reclusive, spending most of its time inside or just at the entrance of its burrow, and is most active during low-light periods.

The loud snapping that gives pistol shrimp their common name is produced by a specialised enlarged claw that creates a cavitation bubble (a tiny, intensely fast water jet that briefly collapses with an audible pop). This is completely normal behaviour used for defence, prey stunning, and communication, and poses no risk to a standard glass aquarium at this body size.

Like all marine crustaceans, the Candy Stripe Pistol Shrimp is sensitive to copper-based medications — a treatment common in marine fish parasite remedies. Any disease treatment containing copper should be carried out in a separate quarantine tank rather than in a reef or mixed invertebrate system. The shrimp is generally considered peaceful toward fish and corals, but the snap is powerful enough to briefly stun small ornamental shrimp, so particularly delicate or tiny shrimp species should be introduced with care.

A tank of at least 75 litres is generally recommended, and a larger system is advisable if a shrimp-goby symbiosis is the goal, since the goby and shrimp will both benefit from more open sand area.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding is not commonly reported. Larval development passes through planktonic stages that are difficult to rear in a home aquarium.

additional.name: FreshMarine — Randall's Pistol Shrimp
additional.notes: Substituted as a secondary because BlueZoo Aquatics returned 403. Cross-checked temperature (72–81°F / 22–27°C), pH (8.10–8.40), specific gravity (1.023–1.025), adult size (~1.5 in / 3.8 cm), copper sensitivity, and goby symbiosis.

additional.name: Aquarium Specialty — Candy Stripe Pistol Shrimp (Alpheus sp.) Learning Center
additional.notes: Confirmed Indo-Pacific range (Red Sea to Central Pacific), carnivorous/omnivorous diet, reef-safe status, and 'up to 5 cm' adult size. Goby symbiosis described in detail.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Alpheus randalli
additional.notes: Taxonomy fallback: Family Alpheidae, Order Decapoda. ~3 cm adult length, originally described from 18 m depth, Indo-Pacific (Marquesas to Seychelles). Used for taxonomy only — not in the hobby source whitelist.

crustacean.moltingNotes: Like other marine shrimp, has been reported to shed its exoskeleton periodically; provide stable calcium, magnesium, and iodine levels to support successful moults.
crustacean.breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably reported; planktonic larval phase makes home rearing impractical.
